key_insight: The paper couples sparse physics-augmented neural constitutive models with FEM topology optimization to jointly optimize material composition and structure in multimaterial 3D printing.
engineering_relevance: This enables end-to-end design of soft robotic and other graded components with composition-aware hyperelastic behavior, reducing reliance on manual prototyping.
method: Extract closed-form, composition-aware hyperelastic laws from experimental data using sparsified physics-augmented neural networks.
Use exact symbolic differentiation and the adjoint method in FEniCSx to drive finite-element topology optimization.
Optimize material distribution and macroscopic topology simultaneously under stretch/non-failure constraints.
evidence_signal: The framework is demonstrated on soft robotic gripper cases, including continuous composition optimization for anisotropic contact response and concurrent topology/material optimization.
watch_item: The approach depends on accurate constitutive identification and a well-integrated adjoint/FEniCSx pipeline, so model mismatch or implementation complexity could limit robustness.
paper_title: Towards end-to-end optimization in multimaterial 3D printing
